questions.specification: Which of the following serves as the primary substrate for the body's energy requirements in an overnight-fasted resting (basal) state?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which nutrient/substrate primarily fuels energy needs at rest/basal state (e.g., lipids vs glucose), which falls under macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Resting/basal energy supply relates to use of oxidative metabolism (fat oxidation vs carbohydrate), which is part of understanding energy production pathways/systems.

options.markscheme: Glucose (carbohydrates) contributes to energy metabolism, but in an overnight-fasted resting state it is typically not the predominant substrate compared with fat oxidation.
In an overnight-fasted resting state, the body predominantly utilizes lipids (fats) for aerobic metabolism, providing the largest proportion of required energy.
Amino acids are primarily used for protein synthesis and tissue maintenance; they contribute a relatively small proportion of energy except during starvation or prolonged exercise.
Protein can be used as an energy source, but it is not the primary substrate in an overnight-fasted resting state under normal conditions.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question links iron levels (affecting hemoglobin/oxygen carrying capacity) to VO₂max and asks who is limited in aerobic performance due to inadequate oxygen transport—this is a key factor affecting maximal oxygen consumption.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.3 Transport > A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Inadequate oxygen transport is primarily a cardiovascular/blood oxygen-carrying issue (hemoglobin/RBC), fitting cardiovascular system function in transport.

options.markscheme: Athlete A has iron levels within the normal range and a high $\text{VO}_2\text{max}$, suggesting their performance is not primarily limited by oxygen transport.

Athlete B has the lowest iron level ($33\,\mu\text{g}\,\text{dL}^{-1}$), which is significantly below typical normal ranges. Iron is essential for the production of hemoglobin, the protein responsible for oxygen transport in the blood. Inadequate iron leads to reduced oxygen transport capacity, which is a major limiting factor for aerobic performance and $\text{VO}_2\text{max}$.
While Athlete C's iron level is slightly lower than A and D, it is significantly higher than Athlete B's, making them less likely to be limited by oxygen transport than B.
Athlete D has iron levels within the normal range and a high $\text{VO}_2\text{max}$.

questions.specification:
Athletes completed 120 minutes of cycling at $70\% \, \text{VO}_2\text{max}$ after following a high-carbohydrate or high-fat diet for 5 days. Figure 1 shows the estimated contribution of fat, muscle glycogen, and plasma glucose oxidation in each condition. Use the values (in g) shown within the bars.

In a separate trial, Figure 2 compares the hourly energy contribution ($\text{kcal}\,\text{h}^{-1}$) of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats during low- and high-intensity exercise.

Researchers explored how macronutrient intake and exercise intensity influence substrate preference and energy efficiency.

Figure 1: Substrate Oxidation After 5-Day High-CHO vs High-Fat Diet

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3qfiRfAGaWcTU0uDI7O2W)

Figure 2: Macronutrient Contribution by Exercise Intensity

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/pWDWs_vtcUmBbDn0NpouS)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on how high-CHO vs high-fat diets and exercise intensity change substrate (fat vs carbohydrate, including muscle glycogen and plasma glucose) oxidation, and asks for performance advantages/disadvantages of these macronutrient strategies.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts involve interpreting substrate use shifts with exercise intensity (low vs high intensity) and linking this to metabolic substrate utilization/efficiency during aerobic exercise, which aligns with oxidative vs glycolytic energy production emphasis.

parts.content: Using Figure 1, describe how the oxidation of fat and muscle glycogen differs between the high-carbohydrate and high-fat diets.
parts.markscheme:
- Fat oxidation is higher in the high-fat diet ($94\text{ g}$) than in the high-carbohydrate diet ($61\text{ g}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Muscle glycogen oxidation is higher in the high-carbohydrate diet ($283\text{ g}$) than in the high-fat diet ($209\text{ g}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept alternative comparative statements using differences (e.g., fat is $33\text{ g}$ higher in high-fat; muscle glycogen is $74\text{ g}$ higher in high-carbohydrate).

parts.content: Refer to Figure 1. Calculate the total carbohydrate oxidation (plasma glucose + muscle glycogen) in grams for each diet condition.
parts.markscheme:
- High-carbohydrate diet: $283\text{ g} + 59\text{ g} = 342\text{ g}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High-fat diet: $209\text{ g} + 62\text{ g} = 271\text{ g}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using your answer to Part 2, explain the potential advantage of high-fat adaptation on glycogen sparing.
parts.markscheme:
- High-fat adaptation increases reliance on fat oxidation and reduces total carbohydrate oxidation (e.g., $271\text{ g}$ vs $342\text{ g}$), lowering the rate of muscle glycogen use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glycogen sparing can delay fatigue by preserving glycogen for later in exercise and/or for periods when intensity increases. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept other valid advantages linked to lower carbohydrate use, such as reduced risk of hypoglycaemia/hitting the wall due to less reliance on glucose.

parts.content: Refer to Figure 2. Identify which macronutrient shows the greatest absolute change in energy contribution between low- and high-intensity exercise.
parts.markscheme: Carbohydrates. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how this reflects a metabolic shift in substrate utilization and efficiency.
parts.markscheme:
- At higher intensity, there is a shift toward carbohydrate use because it can provide ATP at a faster rate (higher power output) via glycolysis/rapid carbohydrate oxidation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Carbohydrate oxidation yields more energy per litre of oxygen than fat (greater oxygen efficiency), which becomes more important as exercise intensity increases and oxygen delivery is limiting. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept another valid efficiency explanation, such as higher intensity recruiting more type II fibres that preferentially use carbohydrate.

parts.content: Outline one advantage of a high-carbohydrate diet for performance and one disadvantage of prolonged high-fat diets in endurance athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage of high-carbohydrate diet: maximizes muscle/liver glycogen stores, supporting higher-intensity performance and/or improving time to exhaustion. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage of prolonged high-fat diet: may reduce the ability to utilize carbohydrates at high intensities (metabolic inflexibility) and/or reduce performance when high rates of glycolysis are required. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept other valid disadvantages, such as reduced capacity for high-intensity surges/finishing sprint due to lower carbohydrate availability.

questions.specification: A healthy gut microbiome can benefit athletic performance by:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.3 Influence of the gut microbiome on health and performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.3 Influence of the gut microbiome on health and performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how a healthy gut microbiome benefits athletic performance (e.g., nutrient absorption, inflammation), which directly matches the HL topic on gut microbiome influence on health and performance.

options.markscheme: Incorrect — glycogen is synthesised in liver and skeletal muscle cells from glucose; gut microbes do not directly produce muscle glycogen.

options.content: Improving nutrient absorption and reducing inflammation
options.markscheme: Correct — a healthy microbiome can support digestion and nutrient absorption and help modulate immune function, reducing chronic inflammation that may impair performance and recovery.

options.content: Increasing haemoglobin concentration and oxygen-carrying capacity
options.markscheme: Incorrect — haemoglobin concentration is primarily influenced by erythropoiesis, iron status, altitude exposure and training adaptations; it is not directly increased by the gut microbiome in a typical, direct way.

options.content: Enhancing motor unit recruitment by speeding nerve impulse conduction
options.markscheme: Incorrect — nerve impulse conduction velocity is mainly determined by myelination and nerve fibre properties; while the gut–brain axis may affect mood/fatigue, a direct increase in conduction speed is not a standard mechanism.

Four pie charts (A–D) show food group selections as percentages (%) of all responses, not individuals. Additionally, researchers recorded the average number of distinct food items ticked per athlete per session (count, unitless), shown as mean number of items selected $ SD (items) in Table 1.

| Group | Pre-Workout | Post-Workout |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| Female athletes | 2.4 $\pm$ 0.6 | 2.1 $\pm$ 0.5 |
| Male athletes | 2.8 $\pm$ 0.7 | 2.3 $\pm$ 0.4 |
  
Figure: Food types selected pre- and post-workout (% of total responses)

A: Females – pre-workout

B: Females – post-workout

parts.content: Identify the most selected food type for males post-workout (Figure D).
parts.markscheme:
- Protein (41.03%) in males post-workout (Figure D)

_**OR**_

- Protein was the most selected food type by male athletes after workouts <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe two key changes in food group preference between female athletes pre- and post-workout.
parts.markscheme:
- Carbohydrate intake decreased: 32.41% (pre)  30.04% (post)
- Protein intake increased: 26.87% (pre)  33.26% (post)
- Fruits and vegetables decreased: 30.47%  24.03%
- Dairy increased: 8.31%  11.37% <marks>2 marks</marks>

<Note> Award 1 mark per valid observation maximum 2 required

parts.content: State one physiological reason why male athletes may prefer higher carbohydrate intake before exercise.
parts.markscheme:
- Carbohydrates provide quick-release energy needed for training intensity

_**OR**_

- Stored as glycogen in muscles and liver to fuel prolonged exercise

_**OR**_

- Males may have greater absolute energy demands due to larger muscle mass <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Use Table 1 to calculate the average reduction in the number of food items selected from pre- to post-workout for each sex.
parts.markscheme:
Female: $2.4 - 2.1 = 0.3$ fewer items post-workout <marks>1 mark</marks>

Male: $2.8 - 2.3 = 0.5$ fewer items post-workout <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

Female reduction stated as $0.3$ items; male reduction stated as $0.5$ items (with or without working shown) <marks>2 marks</marks>

_**OR**_

Accept working shown as post  pre giving negative changes: female $2.1 - 2.4 = -0.3$; male $2.3 - 2.8 = -0.5$ (interpreted as reductions of 0.3 and 0.5 items) <marks>2 marks</marks>

<Note> Award 1 mark per correct sex-based calculation

parts.content: Suggest how a reduced variety of food items post-workout might influence macronutrient balance or recovery.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced variety may lead to macronutrient imbalances post-workout <marks>1 mark</marks>

- May lack sufficient protein or carbohydrate to support glycogen replenishment and muscle recovery

_**OR**_

- May miss micronutrients from fruits/vegetables that support repair <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award 1 mark for consequence, and 1 mark for physiological rationale

parts.content: Explain how protein consumption after training supports exercise recovery and muscle adaptation.
parts.markscheme:
- Protein supports muscle protein synthesis
- Repairs micro-tears in muscle tissue caused by resistance/impact
- Helps prevent muscle breakdown and promotes adaptation <marks>2 marks</marks>

<Note> Any two

parts.content: Evaluate whether post-workout protein intake appears adequate for both sexes.
parts.markscheme:
- Males: post-workout protein = 41.03%  appears sufficient
- Females: post-workout protein = 33.26%  adequate but lower than males <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Both groups increased protein post-workout compared with pre-workout, aligning with recovery needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Limitation: percentages are of total responses (not individuals) so adequacy cannot be confirmed without absolute intake (e.g., g protein, body mass, total energy) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Adequacy depends on total quantity and timing; the chart only shows relative preference, so conclusions are tentative <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award 1 mark for a data-supported observation, and 1 mark for an evaluation of adequacy/limitation

parts.content: Evaluate how inadequate macronutrient intake relative to energy expenditure could increase the risk of Low Energy Availability (LEA) or Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S). Use evidence from the chart and table to support your explanation.
parts.markscheme:
- Low macronutrient intake post-exercise may lead to Low Energy Availability (LEA)

_**OR**_

- Prolonged LEA can lead to RED-S, affecting bone health, immunity, and hormonal function <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Athletes consuming fewer food items post-exercise (Table 1) may fail to meet recovery energy needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Especially true if workouts are high-intensity and followed by inadequate protein or carbs

- Risk may be higher in females due to lower post-workout item count and lower energy intake overall <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award 1 mark for identifying LEA/RED-S risk, 1 mark for linking it to data, 1 mark for physiological consequence

parts.content: Describe three functions of water during physical activity.
parts.markscheme:
- Regulates body temperature via sweat production and evaporation from the skin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains plasma volume/blood volume to support stroke volume and blood pressure during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transports nutrients/oxygen and removes metabolic waste products (e.g. via blood plasma) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lubricates joints and acts as a shock absorber (synovial fluid) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports digestion/absorption and metabolic reactions (solvent/medium) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how fluid replacement strategies can prevent a decline in performance in endurance events, with reference to the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Replacing fluid limits dehydration and associated reductions in endurance performance (e.g. earlier fatigue/increased perceived exertion) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains plasma/blood volume, supporting stroke volume/cardiac output and oxygen delivery to working muscles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports thermoregulation by allowing continued sweating and limiting excessive rises in core temperature (particularly relevant on Day 1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces cardiovascular drift and helps sustain pace/power output during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using an appropriate drink composition (water vs electrolyte-containing drink) can help maintain sodium balance and reduce risk of hyponatremia when large volumes are consumed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps maintain cognitive function/decision-making and reduces risk of cramping associated with fluid–electrolyte disturbance <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the roles of sodium and potassium in regulating hydration status.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium is the main extracellular cation; helps maintain extracellular osmolality and water retention <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium supports water absorption in the small intestine (sodium–glucose co-transport), improving fluid uptake during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium promotes thirst and helps conserve water via hormonal/kidney mechanisms (e.g. ADH/aldosterone-mediated reabsorption) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potassium is the main intracellular cation; helps maintain intracellular fluid volume/osmolality <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potassium is important for normal nerve impulse transmission and muscle contraction; imbalance can affect fluid distribution and function <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium and potassium gradients maintain osmotic balance across cell membranes and the distribution of water between compartments <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how hydration needs vary depending on environmental conditions and exercise intensity, with reference to the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- In hot/humid conditions (Day 1), sweat rate is higher, increasing total fluid requirements to limit dehydration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher exercise intensity increases metabolic heat production and ventilation, increasing fluid loss via sweat and respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater sweat losses increase electrolyte (especially sodium) losses; longer/hotter sessions may require electrolyte-containing fluids rather than only plain water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In cool conditions (Day 2), thirst can be blunted but fluid is still lost through respiration and sweat, increasing risk of under-drinking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individual variation (body size, fitness, sweat rate, acclimatization) means hydration strategies should be personalized <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pre-hydration status and planned intake opportunities influence needs during the session (access to fluids, pacing, duration) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over-drinking hypotonic fluid without replacing sodium can increase hyponatremia risk during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the contribution of the anaerobic glycolytic (lactic acid) system during a $400\text{ m}$ sprint.

- this process results in the accumulation of lactic acid/lactate in the muscles and blood <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the interplay of energy systems during a $1500\text{ m}$ middle-distance race.
parts.markscheme:
- all three energy systems (ATP-PC, anaerobic glycolytic, and aerobic) contribute simultaneously, shifting in their relative contribution (energy continuum) <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification: Which type of carbohydrate is best to consume immediately before or during competition for rapid energy?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which carbohydrate type provides rapid energy during competition, which falls under macronutrients (carbohydrates) and their role in energy provision for performance.

options.markscheme: Complex carbohydrates consist of long chains of sugar molecules. They take longer to digest and absorb, making them better for sustained energy release rather than immediate energy during a competition.
Simple carbohydrates like glucose have a high glycemic index and are rapidly absorbed into the bloodstream, providing an immediate source of energy for the muscles during high-intensity competition.
Fiber is a type of complex carbohydrate that the body cannot digest. It slows down the absorption of sugar and is not used as a source of rapid energy.
Resistant starch behaves similarly to fiber, passing through the small intestine undigested. It is not an effective source of rapid energy during exercise.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It implicitly involves energy metabolism/fuel utilization (why protein is not preferred compared with carbohydrate/fat), linking to how energy is produced via metabolic systems.

options.content: It cannot be used to provide energy in aerobic metabolism.
options.markscheme: Proteins (amino acids) can be oxidized aerobically, so this statement is incorrect.

options.content: It yields less energy per gram than carbohydrate.
options.markscheme: Protein and carbohydrate both provide about $17\ \text{kJ g}^{-1}$ (4 kcal g$^{-1}$), so this statement is incorrect.

options.content: It produces nitrogenous waste that must be excreted, increasing fluid/renal load.
options.markscheme: Amino acid deamination produces ammonia which is converted to urea; excretion increases nitrogenous waste handling and can contribute to dehydration/renal stress, making protein a poor primary fuel.

options.content: It can be stored as protein in muscles and used later, so it is not available during exercise.
options.markscheme: Protein is not stored as a dedicated energy reserve; muscle protein has functional roles and is not ‘stored for later’ fuel use, so this statement is incorrect.
